seo.title: Ayutthaya Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Ayutthaya Province: UNESCO World Heritage ruins, ancient temples, roti sai mai, and boat noodles. Area: 2,548 km², population: 870,000. Plan your visit to Siam's former capital.

provinceNameThai: พระนครศรีอยุธยา
overview: Ayutthaya Province (Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya) is a central province of Thailand and home to the ruins of one of the most powerful kingdoms in Southeast Asian history. The city of Ayutthaya served as the capital of the Kingdom of Siam from 1350 to 1767, when it was conquered and burned by the Burmese. At its zenith, Ayutthaya was one of the largest cities in the world, with over a million inhabitants and diplomatic ties to France, China, Japan, and Persia. Today, the Ayutthaya Historical Park — a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1991 — preserves the magnificent temple ruins, royal palaces, and monasteries spread across an island formed by the confluence of three rivers: the Chao Phraya, the Pa Sak, and the Lopburi.
geography: Ayutthaya Province covers 2,548 km² of flat alluvial plains in the lower Chao Phraya River basin, roughly 80 km north of Bangkok. The historic city sits on an island at the confluence of three rivers — the Chao Phraya, the Pa Sak, and the Lopburi — which historically made it both highly defensible and prone to seasonal flooding. The province is one of Thailand's most fertile rice-growing regions, with extensive paddy fields surrounding the urban areas. The terrain is entirely lowland with no significant elevation changes, typical of the central plains.
highlights: Ayutthaya Historical Park — UNESCO World Heritage Site with hundreds of temple ruins from the 14th–18th century Siamese capital
Wat Mahathat — iconic Buddha head entwined in the roots of a Bodhi tree, one of Thailand's most photographed images
Wat Chaiwatthanaram — a stunning Khmer-style temple complex on the banks of the Chao Phraya, particularly beautiful at sunset
Bang Pa-In Royal Palace — a lavish summer palace with Thai, Chinese, Gothic, and Victorian architectural styles, still used by the Royal Family
Floating Market — Ayothaya Floating Market offers boat-based shopping and dining in a traditional canal setting
Roti Sai Mai street — Ayutthaya's signature sweet treat, cotton candy wrapped in paper-thin roti, sold along Uthong Road
Boat tours around the island city — evening cruises along the three rivers with illuminated temple views
Cycling through the ruins — the flat terrain and compact historic area make Ayutthaya one of Thailand's best cycling destinations

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool season with temperatures of 20–32°C. Clear skies and comfortable humidity. The Ayutthaya World Heritage Fair in December draws large crowds. Best weather for cycling through the ruins.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures reaching 35–40°C. The heat can make outdoor temple exploration uncomfortable. Songkran (April 13–15) is celebrated enthusiastically. Fewer tourists and lower hotel prices.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Rainy season with regular afternoon showers. October is the peak flooding month — the low-lying province is historically flood-prone (severe flooding in 2011). Some temple grounds may be partially submerged. Hotel prices drop significantly.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the most comfortable weather. The ruins are best explored early morning or late afternoon to avoid midday heat. Avoid October if possible due to flood risk.

topAttractions.name: Wat Mahathat
topAttractions.description: One of Ayutthaya's oldest and most significant temples, founded in 1374 during the reign of King Borommaracha I. Most famous for the sandstone Buddha head entwined in the roots of a Bodhi tree — one of Thailand's most iconic images. The temple once held the kingdom's most important relic of the Buddha. Extensive ruins of prangs and chedis remain.

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Si Sanphet
topAttractions.description: The holiest temple of the Ayutthaya Kingdom, located within the grounds of the former Royal Palace. Its three iconic bell-shaped chedis — containing the ashes of three Ayutthayan kings — are the defining symbol of the ancient capital. Once home to a 16-meter standing Buddha covered in 250 kg of gold, melted down by the Burmese invaders in 1767.

topAttractions.name: Wat Chaiwatthanaram
topAttractions.description: A stunning Khmer-style temple built in 1630 by King Prasat Thong on the west bank of the Chao Phraya River. The central 35-meter prang surrounded by eight smaller chedis mirrors the layout of Angkor Wat. Particularly photogenic at sunset when the warm light illuminates the sandstone ruins against the river backdrop.

topAttractions.name: Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon
topAttractions.description: Built in 1357 by King U-Thong, this temple features a massive chedi (62 meters) that can be climbed for panoramic views of the surrounding rice fields. A large reclining Buddha draped in saffron robes sits in the grounds. The temple was built to celebrate King Naresuan's victory over the Burmese in single elephant-back combat in 1593.

topAttractions.name: Wat Ratchaburana
topAttractions.description: Built in 1424 by King Borommaracha II on the cremation site of his two older brothers who died fighting each other for the throne. The impressive Khmer-style prang still stands tall and contains a crypt with original murals — some of the oldest surviving paintings in Thailand. Gold artifacts found here are displayed in the Chao Sam Phraya National Museum.

topAttractions.name: Bang Pa-In Royal Palace
topAttractions.description: A lavish summer palace complex 20 km south of Ayutthaya city, originally built by King Prasat Thong in the 17th century and restored by King Chulalongkorn (Rama V) in the late 19th century. The palace showcases a remarkable mix of Thai, Chinese, Gothic, and Victorian architectural styles. The Aisawan Thiphya-Art pavilion in the middle of the lake is one of Thailand's most photographed buildings.

topAttractions.name: Chao Sam Phraya National Museum
topAttractions.description: Ayutthaya's principal museum housing artifacts recovered from the ruins, including gold treasures found in the crypt of Wat Ratchaburana, Buddha images, ceramics, and royal regalia. Provides essential historical context for understanding the Ayutthayan kingdom's 417-year reign.

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Ram
topAttractions.description: One of the oldest temples in Ayutthaya, built in 1369 on the cremation site of King U-Thong (Ramathibodi I), the founder of the Ayutthayan kingdom. Set beside a scenic lake, the main corn-cob shaped Khmer prang and surrounding grounds offer a peaceful, less-crowded alternative to the more popular temples.

localFood: Roti Sai Mai — Ayutthaya's signature sweet: colorful strands of spun palm sugar (cotton candy) wrapped in paper-thin roti flatbread. Best found along Uthong Road near Ayutthaya Hospital, where vendors have been making it for generations

Boat Noodles (Kuay Tiao Ruea) — Ayutthaya's most famous savory dish, originating from canal vendors. Tiny bowls of rice noodles in a rich, dark pork broth with blood curd, liver, and crispy pork skin. Only 15–20 THB per bowl — most people eat 5–10 bowls
Khao Mok Kai — yellow turmeric rice with chicken, a Muslim-Thai dish reflecting Ayutthaya's historic multicultural community. Served with sweet dipping sauce and cucumber relish
Goong Mae Nam (giant river prawns) — freshwater prawns grilled over charcoal, a local specialty from the Chao Phraya and Pa Sak rivers. Best from October to March
Pla Chon Lui Suan (snakehead fish with herbs) — deep-fried snakehead fish topped with a spicy herb salad, a central plains delicacy
Khanom Buang — crispy Thai crepes filled with sweet coconut cream and savory shredded shrimp, a popular street snack throughout the old city
culture: Ayutthaya was the capital of the Kingdom of Siam for 417 years (1350–1767), during which 33 kings from 5 dynasties ruled over one of the most cosmopolitan cities in the world. At its peak in the 17th century, Ayutthaya had a population exceeding one million and maintained foreign quarters for Japanese, Chinese, Portuguese, Dutch, French, and Persian merchants and diplomats. The city was a center of Buddhist learning, art, and architecture, blending Khmer, Sukhothai, and local Siamese styles into a distinctive artistic tradition. The devastating Burmese sack of 1767 destroyed most of the city, but the ruins — with their headless Buddha statues and crumbling prangs — remain a powerful reminder of the kingdom's former glory. The province's Muslim community, dating back centuries, contributes to its unique food culture, particularly roti sai mai and khao mok kai.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, Ayutthaya is one of the easiest day trips in Thailand. Trains from Bangkok's Bang Sue Grand Station or Hua Lamphong run hourly and take 1.5–2 hours (20–345 THB depending on class). Minivans from Victory Monument take 1–1.5 hours (60–80 THB). Driving takes about 1 hour via Highway 1 or the expressway (80 km). Day trip tours from Bangkok start at 800–1,500 THB including transport, guide, and lunch. River cruises from Bangkok take 3–4 hours upstream and offer a scenic alternative.
gettingAround: The historic island city is compact and best explored by bicycle (50–100 THB/day rental from guesthouses and shops near the train station). Tuk-tuks are widely available — negotiate a half-day or full-day rate (200–300 THB for a 4-hour circuit of major temples). A combined temple pass covering 6 main sites costs 220 THB and saves money over individual entry fees. Songthaews (shared trucks) run fixed routes for 10–15 THB. Long-tail boats can be hired for river tours around the island (500–1,000 THB per hour).
travelTips: Buy the 220 THB combined temple pass if visiting more than 4 temples — it covers the 6 main sites and saves money over individual 50 THB entries
Start temple visits early morning (8 AM) or late afternoon (after 3 PM) to avoid the midday heat, which can be brutal on shadeless temple grounds
Rent a bicycle for the most enjoyable way to explore — the island is flat, distances between temples are short, and car-free paths connect many sites
Dress modestly at all temples — cover shoulders and knees. Carry a light scarf or sarong in your bag
Bring plenty of water and sunscreen — most temple ruins have no shade and limited facilities
The night-lit temples (especially Wat Chaiwatthanaram and Wat Ratchaburana) are stunning — consider staying overnight rather than doing a day trip
Avoid October and early November — the low-lying province is flood-prone, and water levels can rise dramatically after heavy rains
Combine Ayutthaya with a stop at Bang Pa-In Royal Palace, which is on the train route 20 km south of the city
